name: Foundations of Data Science
description: The UC Berkeley Foundations of Data Science course combines three perspectives: inferential thinking, computational thinking, and real-world relevance. Given data arising from some real-world phenomenon, how does one analyze that data so as to understand that phenomenon? The course teaches critical concepts and skills in computer programming and statistical inference, in conjunction with hands-on analysis of real-world datasets, including economic data, document collections, geographical data, and social networks.

projects.name: World Population & Poverty
projects.description: Analyzed global demographic and poverty trends using Gapminder's Systema Globalis data to reveal drivers of negative population growth.
highlights: Synthesized life expectancy, fertility, and child mortality metrics
Engineered Python-based automation tools for calculating poverty by country/year
Designed interactive visualizations comparing regional and temporal trends
Highlighted the 85% to 9% drop in global poverty (1800–2018)

projects.name: Climate Change Analysis
projects.description: Analyzed historical temperature and precipitation records from 210 U.S. cities with statistical inference.
highlights: Engineered geospatial algorithms to classify cities by U.S. region
Developed distance-based methods to identify cross-regional climate patterns
Performed statistical inference with 99% confidence intervals
Conducted A/B test with p-value of 0.026 showing significant rainfall differences

projects.name: Movie Classification
projects.description: Engineered k-nearest neighbors classifier to predict comedy vs. thriller genre from movie screenplays.
highlights: Used bag-of-words representation from 333 movie screenplays (5,000 features)
Performed data cleaning, stemming, and feature selection
Reduced dimensionality to 10 most discriminative features
Achieved 72% classification accuracy on test set

name: Computational Structures in Data Science
description: DATA C88C expands on foundational concepts from Data C8 by diving deeper into programming, algorithms, and system design. It focuses on managing complexity through functional and object-oriented programming paradigms and building larger systems via projects. The course serves as a bridge for deeper Computer Science and Data Science study.

projects.name: Yelp Maps Visualization
projects.description: Created a visualization of restaurant scores using machine learning and Yelp academic dataset.
highlights: Segmented Berkeley into regions using Voronoi diagrams
Each region shaded by predicted score (yellow = 5 stars, blue = 1 star)
Implemented user preference modeling for location-based recommendations

projects.name: Ants vs. SomeBees (Tower Defense)
projects.description: Created a tower defense game inspired by Plants vs. Zombies using object-oriented programming.
highlights: Implemented object-oriented programming paradigm
Built class hierarchies with inheritance for different ant and bee types
Extended and tested a large codebase

name: Linear Algebra
description: A rigorous introduction to linear algebra covering vectors, matrices, linear transformations, systems of equations, and applications. The course builds mathematical maturity and provides essential foundations for machine learning, computer graphics, and data science applications.

name: Introduction to Computational Techniques in Physics
description: An intensive introduction to computational methods in physics, covering Python programming, numerical methods, data analysis, and scientific computing. Culminates in a research-level group project applying computational techniques to physics problems.

projects.name: Lift and Drag Calculations with 2D Finite Difference Navier–Stokes Solver
projects.description: Developed a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) solver for 2D Navier-Stokes equations as a research paper.
highlights: Calculated lift and drag forces on objects using finite difference methods
Group research project applying numerical methods to physics simulation
Published research paper on computational fluid dynamics

name: Data and Decisions
description: UGBA 88 teaches students how data analysis, statistics, and programming (Python, SQL) improve business and managerial decisions. The course covers causal inference, experiments, regression, and real-world applications in marketing, labor, and online markets to build data-driven decision-making skills.

name: Data Structures
description: Fundamental dynamic data structures, including linear lists, queues, trees, and graphs. Sorting and searching. Storage management. Data abstraction and software engineering. One of Berkeley's most rigorous CS core courses, covering object-oriented design, testing, and building large-scale software systems in Java.

name: Data and Justice
description: Critical examination of data science practices through the lens of social justice, ethics, and fairness. Explores how data systems impact communities, how algorithmic decision-making can perpetuate structural inequalities, and how to build more equitable and accountable technologies.

projects.name: Algorithmic Bias and Ethics in AI Research Paper
projects.description: Original research paper examining ethical challenges of AI decision-making systems, focusing on bias in hiring, criminal justice, and finance.
highlights: Analyzed how biased algorithms lead to discrimination in high-stakes domains
Combined academic analysis with rhetorical poetry for emotional perspectives
Argued for transparency, fairness, and human oversight in AI development

name: Principles & Techniques of Data Science
description: Advanced data science techniques covering the full pipeline from data collection and wrangling to modeling and inference. Combines inferential and computational thinking at scale, with emphasis on real-world datasets, reproducible workflows, and rigorous statistical reasoning.

name: Fashion x Data Science
description: A student-run DeCal (Democratic Education at Cal) exploring the intersection of fashion and data science. Covers machine learning applications in trend forecasting, image recognition for style classification, recommendation systems, and sustainable fashion analytics. Combines creative design thinking with rigorous data science methodology.

name: Calculus — Complete Sequence
description: Full calculus sequence built across AP courses, dual enrollment at Coastline Community College, and an upcoming Summer 2026 multivariable course. Calculus 1 (AP AB): Score 5 — only student at West Valley High School to pass that year (school's 7% pass rate). Calculus 1/2 (AP BC): Score 5 — extending single-variable mastery to series, sequences, and integration techniques. Calculus 2 (MATH C185 at Coastline CC): A+ — rigorous treatment of integration methods, series convergence, and parametric/polar curves. Calculus 3 / Multivariable Calculus (MATH C280 at Coastline CC): Upcoming Summer 2026 — equivalent to UC Berkeley MATH 53, transferable, and a prerequisite for upper-division machine learning, probability theory, and optimization coursework.
topics: Series and Sequences (Taylor, Maclaurin, convergence tests)

projects.name: AP Calculus AB — Score 5
projects.description: Completed AP Calculus AB with a perfect score of 5 at West Valley High School (Hemet, CA), where only 7% of students passed AP Calculus that year. Self-studied without a strong AP prep culture or paid tutoring.
highlights: Only student at WVHS to pass AP Calculus that year
School AP Calculus pass rate: 7%
Covers limits, derivatives, and basic integration

projects.name: AP Calculus BC — Score 5
projects.description: Completed AP Calculus BC with a perfect score of 5, extending single-variable mastery to sequences, series, and advanced integration.
highlights: Score 5 (highest possible) on the BC exam
Covers all of AB content plus series, sequences, polar, and parametric
Builds the Taylor series foundation used directly in ML theory

projects.name: MATH C185 — Calculus 2 at Coastline CC (A+)
projects.description: Completed Calculus 2 (MATH C185) at Coastline Community College as dual enrollment with an A+. Rigorous college-level treatment of integration, series convergence, and applications.
highlights: A+ grade in a 5-unit college calculus course
Integration by parts, trigonometric substitution, partial fractions
Infinite series, convergence tests, and power series

projects.name: MATH C280 — Calculus 3 / Multivariable at Coastline CC (Summer 2026)
projects.description: Upcoming Summer 2026. Multivariable calculus covering 3D geometry, partial derivatives, gradient, multiple integrals, and vector calculus theorems. Equivalent to UC Berkeley MATH 53 and transferable for upper-division EECS, Data Science, and Statistics prerequisites.
highlights: Equivalent to Berkeley MATH 53 (transferable credit)
Required for upper-division ML, probability, and optimization courses
Covers the mathematical foundation for gradient descent, Jacobians, and Hessians used in ML

name: Data Engineering
description: Covers the design, implementation, and management of data pipelines and infrastructure at scale. Topics include data storage systems, ETL workflows, stream processing, and cloud-based data architectures. Builds the engineering foundation for production data science systems.

name: Human Contexts and Ethics of Data
description: Interdisciplinary course examining how data and algorithms shape society, culture, and power. Draws from history, science and technology studies (STS), and critical data studies to analyze the social contexts and ethical dimensions of data-driven systems.

name: Discrete Math and Probability Theory
description: Foundational course in discrete mathematics and probability theory for computer scientists. Covers proof techniques, number theory, graph theory, polynomials over finite fields, and probability — essential preparation for upper-division algorithms, ML theory, and cryptography courses.
